q: When the wavelength of radiation falling on a metal is changed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mi>nm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>to</mi><mo> </mo><mn>200</mn><mi>nm</mi></math>, the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons becomes three times larger. The work function of the metal is close to:
question: When the wavelength of radiation falling on a metal is changed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mi>nm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>to</mi><mo> </mo><mn>200</mn><mi>nm</mi></math>, the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons becomes three times larger. The work function of the metal is close to:
solution:
KEmax=hcλ-ϕ=hc500-ϕ   .....(i)

Now,

3KEmax=hc200-ϕ

   .....(ii)

From equation (i) and (ii)

iii=31=hc200-ϕhc500-ϕ

Put the value of hc=1237.5 and solving ϕ=0.61eV

options: Both metals <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> will emit photo-electrons

solution:
As we know, for photoelectric emission, hν>ϕ.

Now,

hν=hcλ=1240350 eV=3.54 eV

Therefore, only metal B will emit photoelectrons.

solution:
hλ=hλx-hλy
1λ=1λx-1λy
λ=λxλyλx-λy

q: The stopping potential for electrons emitted from a photosensitive surface illuminated by light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>491</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>710</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> When the incident wavelength is changed to a new value, the stopping potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>43</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The new wavelength is :
question: The stopping potential for electrons emitted from a photosensitive surface illuminated by light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>491</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>710</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> When the incident wavelength is changed to a new value, the stopping potential is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>43</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The new wavelength is :
solution:
hcλ=ϕ+eVs

1240491=ϕ+0.71      ...1

1240λ=ϕ+1.43      ...2

∴λ=382 nm

subject: Physics
q: The de Broglie wavelengths of a proton and an α particle are λ and 2λ respectively. The ratio of the velocities of proton and α particle will be :
question: The de Broglie wavelengths of a proton and an α particle are λ and 2λ respectively. The ratio of the velocities of proton and α particle will be :
solution:
As we know, de Broglie wavelength is given by, λ=hp=hmv

⇒v=hmλ

Therefore, 

vpvα=mαmp×λαλp

=4×2=8

question: If the kinetic energy of a free electron doubles. Its deBroglie wavelength changes by the factor

question: The work function of a substance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math>. The longest wavelength of light that can cause the emission of photoelectrons from this substance is approximately:
solution:
For longest wavelength, the emitted electron will have nearly zero kinetic energy.

Therefore,

λ=hcWe

⇒λ=1240 nm-eV3 eV

⇒λ=413.33 nm

⇒λmax≈414 nm

subject: Physics
q: When photons of wavelength <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> are incident on an isolated sphere, the corresponding stopping potential is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math>. When photons of wavelength <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> are used, the corresponding stopping potential was thrice that of the above value. If light of wavelength <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> is used then find the stopping potential for this case:
question: When photons of wavelength <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> are incident on an isolated sphere, the corresponding stopping potential is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math>. When photons of wavelength <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> are used, the corresponding stopping potential was thrice that of the above value. If light of wavelength <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> is used then find the stopping potential for this case:
solution:
hcλ1=hcλ0+eV ...(i)

hcλ2=hcλ0+3eV ...(ii)

hcλ3=hcλ0+eV′ ....(iii)

Equation (i) and (ii) 

32λ1-12λ2=1λ0

hcλ3-hc32λ1-12λ2=eV′

hce1λ3-32λ1+12λ2=V′

subject: Physics
q:
Match the LIST-I with LIST-II
List-IList-II
A. Planck's constantI. $ML^2T^{-2}$
B. Stopping potentialII. $T^{-1}$
C. Work functionIII. $ML^2T^{-1}$
D. Threshold frequencyIV. $ML^2T^{-3}A^{-1}$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match the LIST-I with LIST-II
List-IList-II
A. Planck's constantI. $ML^2T^{-2}$
B. Stopping potentialII. $T^{-1}$
C. Work functionIII. $ML^2T^{-1}$
D. Threshold frequencyIV. $ML^2T^{-3}A^{-1}$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The dimensional formula for Planck's constant $h$ is obtained from $E = h\nu$.

$[h] = \dfrac{[E]}{[\nu]} = \dfrac{ML^2T^{-2}}{T^{-1}} = ML^2T^{-1}$

The dimensional formula for stopping potential $V_0$ is obtained from $eV_0 = E$.

$[V_0] = \dfrac{[E]}{[e]} = \dfrac{ML^2T^{-2}}{AT} = ML^2T^{-3}A^{-1}$

Work function is a form of energy, so its dimensional formula is the same as that of energy.

$[\text{Work function}] = ML^2T^{-2}$

Threshold frequency has the dimensional formula of frequency.

$[\text{Threshold frequency}] = T^{-1}$

Therefore, the correct matching is A-III, B-IV, C-I, D-II.

Answer: A-III, B-IV, C-I, D-II

question: A photocell is illuminated by a small bright source placed $1 \mathrm{~m}$ away. When the same source of light is placed $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}$ away, the number of electrons emitted by photo cathode would

subject: Physics
q: Light is incident normally on a completely absorbing surface with an energy flux of <math><mi> </mi><mn>25</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> . If the surface has an area of <math><mi> </mi><mn>25</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> , the momentum transferred to the surface in <math> <mrow> <mtext>40</mtext><mtext> </mtext><mtext>min</mtext></mrow> </math> time duration will be:
question: Light is incident normally on a completely absorbing surface with an energy flux of <math><mi> </mi><mn>25</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> . If the surface has an area of <math><mi> </mi><mn>25</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> , the momentum transferred to the surface in <math> <mrow> <mtext>40</mtext><mtext> </mtext><mtext>min</mtext></mrow> </math> time duration will be:
solution:
The power of the incident light is P=25  W cm-2×25 cm2
=625 W
Energy incident per second, E=hcλ=625 J
Momentum incident per second, p=Ec=hλ=625c
∴ Total momentum transferred to the surface =625c×40×60
=5×10-3 N s

subject: Physics
q: When a light of a given wavelength falls on a metallic surface the stopping potential for photoelectrons is 3.2 V. If a second light having wavelength twice of first light is used, the stopping potential drops to 0.7 V. The wavelength of first light is $\_\_\_\_$ m. $\left(\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right)$
question: When a light of a given wavelength falls on a metallic surface the stopping potential for photoelectrons is 3.2 V. If a second light having wavelength twice of first light is used, the stopping potential drops to 0.7 V. The wavelength of first light is $\_\_\_\_$ m. $\left(\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}. \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right)$
solution:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equation with stopping potentials:
For first light, $h u_1 = \phi + eV_{s1}$.
For second light with $\lambda_2 = 2\lambda_1$ (so $ u_2 = u_1/2$): $h u_1/2 = \phi + eV_{s2}$.
From given values: $h u_1 - 3.2e = h u_1/2 - 0.7e$ gives $h u_1/2 = 2.5e$.
Thus $\lambda_1 = rac{hc}{5e} = rac{6.63 imes 10^{-34} imes 3 imes 10^8}{5 imes 1.6 imes 10^{-19}} \approx 2.5 imes 10^{-7}$ m.

q:
The electric field at a point associated with a light wave is given by
E=200sin6×1015t+sin9×1015t Vm-1

Given: h=4.14×10-15 eVs

If this light falls on a metal surface having a work function of 2.50 eV, the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons will be
question:
The electric field at a point associated with a light wave is given by
E=200sin6×1015t+sin9×1015t Vm-1

Given: h=4.14×10-15 eVs

If this light falls on a metal surface having a work function of 2.50 eV, the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons will be
solution:
Given: E=200sin6×1015t+sin9×1015t V m-1

Here, angular velocity, ω1=6×1015 and ω2=9×1015

Maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectron is KEmax.=E-ϕ

=hf-ϕ=hω2π-ϕ

=4.14×10-15×9×10152×3.14-2.5=5.92-2.50=3.42 eV

subject: Physics
q:
A light beam of wavelength 500 nm is incident on a metal having work function of 1.25 eV, placed in a magnetic field of intensity B. The electrons emitted perpendicular to the magnetic field B, with maximum kinetic energy are bent into a circular arc of radius 30 cm. The value of B is ________×10-7 T.
Given hc=20×10-26 J m, the mass of the electron=9×10-31 kg.
question:
A light beam of wavelength 500 nm is incident on a metal having work function of 1.25 eV, placed in a magnetic field of intensity B. The electrons emitted perpendicular to the magnetic field B, with maximum kinetic energy are bent into a circular arc of radius 30 cm. The value of B is ________×10-7 T.
Given hc=20×10-26 J m, the mass of the electron=9×10-31 kg.
solution:
By photoelectric equation

hcλ-ϕ=kmax

kmax=1240500-1.25≈1.25

r=2mkeB

B=2mker

=125×10-7 T

subject: Physics
q:
The magnetic field associated with a light wave is given, at the origin, by B=B0 sin⁡3.14×107ct+sin⁡6.28×107ct. If this light falls on a silver plate having a work function of 4.7eV, what will be the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons?

(c=3×108 m s−1,h=6.6×10−34 J s)
question:
The magnetic field associated with a light wave is given, at the origin, by B=B0 sin⁡3.14×107ct+sin⁡6.28×107ct. If this light falls on a silver plate having a work function of 4.7eV, what will be the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons?

(c=3×108 m s−1,h=6.6×10−34 J s)
solution:
B=B0sin⁡3.14×107ct+sin⁡6.28×107ct

Maximum KE will associate with the maximum frequency

fmax⁡ =6.28×107c2π=107×3×108 Hz           =3×1015 Hz

KEmax=hfmax-ϕ

=6.63×10-34×3×10151.6×10-19-4.7 eV

=7.72 eV

subject: Physics
q: An electron of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and a proton of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1836</mn><mo> </mo><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> are moving with the same speed. The ratio of their de Broglie wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>λ</mi><mrow><mi>e</mi><mi>l</mi><mi>e</mi><mi>c</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>r</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>r</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>n</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msub></mfrac></math> will be :
question: An electron of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and a proton of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>p</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1836</mn><mo> </mo><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> are moving with the same speed. The ratio of their de Broglie wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>λ</mi><mrow><mi>e</mi><mi>l</mi><mi>e</mi><mi>c</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>r</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>r</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>n</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msub></mfrac></math> will be :
solution:
Given mass of electron =me
Mass of proton =mp
∴ given mp=1836 me
From de-Broglie wavelength
λ=hp=hmv
λeλp=mpme
=1836 meme
λeλp=1836

question: A radiation of energy $E$ falls normally on a perfectly reflecting surface. The momentum transferred to the surface is

subject: Physics
q: Consider an electron in a hydrogen atom, revolving in its second excited state (having radius <math><mn>4.65</mn><mi> </mi><mtext>Å</mtext></math> ). The de-Broglie wavelength of this electron is:
question: Consider an electron in a hydrogen atom, revolving in its second excited state (having radius <math><mn>4.65</mn><mi> </mi><mtext>Å</mtext></math> ). The de-Broglie wavelength of this electron is:
solution:
According to Bohr’s atomic model
mvr=nh2π
And the de-Broglie wavelength
λ=hmv
From the above two equations we can write, λ=2πrn
In the 2nd excited state
λ=2πr3
∴  λ=23π×4.65  Å=9.7 Å

question: An electron and proton are separated by a large distance. The electron starts approaching the proton with energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>eV</mi></math>. The proton captures the electrons and forms a hydrogen atom in second excited state. The resulting photon is incident on a photosensitive metal of threshold wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4000</mn><mi>A</mi></math> What is the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectron?
solution:
Initially, energy of electron =+3eV

finally, in 2nd  excited state,

energy of electron =-(13.6eV)32

=-1.51eV

Loss in energy is emitted as photon,

So, photon energy hcλ=4.51eV

Now, photoelectric effect equation

KEmax=hcλ-ϕ=4.51-hcλth

=4.51eV-12400eVA4000A

=1.41eV

subject: Physics
q: The graph shows variation of stopping potential $V_o$ with the frequency $\nu$ of the incident radiation for three photosensitive metals $X_1$, $X_2$ and $X_3$. Which metal will give out electrons with greater kinetic energy, for the same wavelength of incident radiation?
question: The graph shows variation of stopping potential $V_o$ with the frequency $\nu$ of the incident radiation for three photosensitive metals $X_1$, $X_2$ and $X_3$. Which metal will give out electrons with greater kinetic energy, for the same wavelength of incident radiation?
options: All the metals will give out photo electrons with same kinetic energies.
solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons is given by:

$K_{ ext{max}} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$

where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of incident radiation and $\Phi$ is the work function of the metal.

The work function is related to the threshold frequency $ u_0$ by $\Phi = h u_0$. From the given graph, the x-intercept represents the threshold frequency. We can observe that:

$ u_0(X_1) < u_0(X_2) < u_0(X_3)$

Therefore, the work functions of the metals follow the order:

$\Phi(X_1) < \Phi(X_2) < \Phi(X_3)$

For the same wavelength $\lambda$ of incident radiation, the term $\dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ is constant. Thus, the kinetic energy $K_{ ext{max}}$ will be greatest for the metal with the smallest work function.

Since metal $X_1$ has the minimum work function, it will emit electrons with the greatest kinetic energy.

Answer: $X_1$

subject: Physics
q:
In photoelectric effect
A. The photocurrent is proportional to the intensity of the incident radiation.
B. Maximum kinetic energy with which photoelectrons are emitted depends on the intensity of incident light.
C. Max K.E. with which photoelectrons are emitted depends on the frequency of incident light.
D. The emission of photoelectrons require a minimum threshold intensity of incident radiation.
E. Max K.E. of the photoelectrons is independent of the frequency of the incident light.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
In photoelectric effect
A. The photocurrent is proportional to the intensity of the incident radiation.
B. Maximum kinetic energy with which photoelectrons are emitted depends on the intensity of incident light.
C. Max K.E. with which photoelectrons are emitted depends on the frequency of incident light.
D. The emission of photoelectrons require a minimum threshold intensity of incident radiation.
E. Max K.E. of the photoelectrons is independent of the frequency of the incident light.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
With proper energetic radiation, the photoelectric current increases with an increase in accelerating potential until it reaches a point where it reaches its maximum and does not increase with an additional increase in accelerating potential.

For a given potential difference, the photocurrent is directly proportional to the intensity of the incident radiation.

The maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons can be written as

Kmax=hν-φ

From above equation, it can be seen that the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectron depends on the frequency of the light used.

Hence, the statements A and C are correct.

q: The speed of electrons in a scanning electron microscope is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the protons having the same speed are used instead of electrons, then the resolving power of scanning proton microscope will be changed by a factor of:
question: The speed of electrons in a scanning electron microscope is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the protons having the same speed are used instead of electrons, then the resolving power of scanning proton microscope will be changed by a factor of:
solution:
Resolving power RP∝1λ

λ=hP=hmv

So, RP∝mvh
RP∝P

RP∝mv

RP∝m

q:
The surface of certain metal is first illuminated with light of wavelength λ1=350 nm and then, by a light of wavelength λ2=540 nm. It is found that the maximum speed of the photoelectrons in the two cases differ by a factor of 2. The work function of the metal (in eV) is close to

(Energy of photon =1240λin nm eV )
question:
The surface of certain metal is first illuminated with light of wavelength λ1=350 nm and then, by a light of wavelength λ2=540 nm. It is found that the maximum speed of the photoelectrons in the two cases differ by a factor of 2. The work function of the metal (in eV) is close to

(Energy of photon =1240λin nm eV )
solution:
λ1=350 nm,  λ2=540 nm,  V1V2=2⇒V1=2V and V2=V

hcλ1=ϕ+12m2V2

hcλ2=ϕ+12mV2

hcλ1-4hcλ2=ϕ-4ϕ

hc4540-1340=3ϕ

ϕ=1.8 eV

subject: Physics
q: The de Broglie wavelengths for an electron and a photon are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> respectively. For the same kinetic energy of electron and photon, which of the following presents the correct relation between the de Broglie wavelengths of two?
question: The de Broglie wavelengths for an electron and a photon are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> respectively. For the same kinetic energy of electron and photon, which of the following presents the correct relation between the de Broglie wavelengths of two?
solution:
De-Broglie wavelength for electron is given by, λe=h2mk

For photon k=hcλp⇒λp=hck. Therefore,

λe=hλp2mhc

λp∝λe2

subject: Physics
q: A proton of mass ' $m_{\mathrm{p}}$ ' has same energy as that of a photon of wavelength ' $\lambda$ '. If the proton is moving at non-relativistic speed, then ratio of its de Broglie wavelength to the wavelength of photon is.
question: A proton of mass ' $m_{\mathrm{p}}$ ' has same energy as that of a photon of wavelength ' $\lambda$ '. If the proton is moving at non-relativistic speed, then ratio of its de Broglie wavelength to the wavelength of photon is.
solution:
Energy of photon $=E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}$
$\Rightarrow$ Wavelength of photon $=\lambda=\frac{h c}{E}$
Energy of proton $=E=\frac{1}{2} m_p v^2=\frac{P^2}{2 m_p}$
$\Rightarrow$ Linear momentum of proton $=P=\sqrt{2 m_p E}$
Or de-Broglie wavelength of proton
$\begin{aligned}
=\lambda_p= & \frac{h}{P}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_p E}} \\
\text { Ratio } \frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda} & =\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_p E}} \times \frac{E}{h c} \\
& =\frac{1}{c} \sqrt{\frac{E}{2 m_p}}
\end{aligned}$

q: In a photoelectric experiment, increasing the intensity of incident light:
question: In a photoelectric experiment, increasing the intensity of incident light:
options: increases the number of photons incident and also increases the K.E. of the ejected electrons.
increases the frequency of photons incident and increases the K.E. of the ejected electrons.
increases the number of photons incident and the K.E. of the ejected electrons remains unchanged.
increases the frequency of photons incident and the K.E. of the ejected electrons remains unchanged.
solution: The relation between the energy of photons and the intensity of photons is, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>I</mi><mi>A</mi></mrow><mi>n</mi></mfrac></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is the number of photons, if we increase the number of photons then frequency does not change it means total energy and kinetic energy of photon remains unchanged but due to to this property the intensity of light is increased.

subject: Physics
q:
When UV light of wavelength $300 \mathrm{~nm}$ is incident on the metal surface having work function $2.13 \mathrm{eV}$, electron emission takes place. The stopping potential is:
(Given hc $=1240 \mathrm{eV} \mathrm{nm}$ )
question:
When UV light of wavelength $300 \mathrm{~nm}$ is incident on the metal surface having work function $2.13 \mathrm{eV}$, electron emission takes place. The stopping potential is:
(Given hc $=1240 \mathrm{eV} \mathrm{nm}$ )
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-\phi=\mathrm{e} . \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1240}{300} \mathrm{eV}-2.13 \mathrm{eV}=\mathrm{eVs} \\ & \Rightarrow 4.13 \mathrm{eV}-2.13 \mathrm{eV}=\mathrm{eVs} . \\ & \Rightarrow \text { So, } \quad \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}=2 \mathrm{volt}\end{aligned}$

question: What should be the order of arrangement of de-Broglie wavelength of electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub></mfenced></math>, an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi></msub></mfenced></math> and proton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></msub></mfenced></math> given that all have the same kinetic energy ?
solution:
λ=hp=h2mE∝1m

mα>mp>me

so λe>λp>λα

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I : Stopping potential in photoelectric effect does not depend on the power of the light source.

Statement II : For a given metal, the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectron depends on the wavelength of the incident light.

In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I : Stopping potential in photoelectric effect does not depend on the power of the light source.

Statement II : For a given metal, the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectron depends on the wavelength of the incident light.

In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: Statement I is incorrect but statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but statement II is incorrect
Both statement I and statement II are correct
solution:
Stopping potential depends on frequency of incident light and nature of the emitter material. It is independent of the power of the light source.

Maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons is stated asKEmax=hν-ϕ=hcλ-ϕ, Where, h is the Planck’s constant, ν is the frequency, λ is wavelength of incident light and ϕ is the work function of the metal surface.

Thus, the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectron depends on the wavelength of the incident light.

So both statements I and II are correct

subject: Physics
q: The de Broglie wavelength of a molecule in a gas at room temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. If the temperature of the gas is increased to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, then the de Broglie wavelength of the same gas molecule becomes
question: The de Broglie wavelength of a molecule in a gas at room temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. If the temperature of the gas is increased to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, then the de Broglie wavelength of the same gas molecule becomes
solution:
The root mean squared velocity of a gas is given by 

v=3RTM

⇒v∝T

Let

 T1=300 KT2=600 K

Taking velocity ratios at the given temperatures,

v1v2=T1T2=300600=12

The de Broglie wavelength is given by λ=hmv. So,

λ∝1v

The ratio of the wavelengths is 

λ1λ2=v2v1=21

⇒λ2=12λ1

q:
If a source of electromagnetic radiation having power 15 kW produces 1016 photons per second, the radiation belongs to a part of spectrum is :

(Take Planck constant h = 6×10-34 J s)
question:
If a source of electromagnetic radiation having power 15 kW produces 1016 photons per second, the radiation belongs to a part of spectrum is :

(Take Planck constant h = 6×10-34 J s)
solution:
As energy of one photon is given by E=PowerPhoton frequency

Or, E=hν=15×103 1016

So, frequency of ray is ν=15×10-136×10-34=2.5×1021 Hz.

Thus, the radiation belongs to gamma rays.
